1â†’--- 2â†’title: Subagents 3â†’description: Delegate context-heavy tasks to specialized subagents while keeping the main agent focused. 4â†’--- 5â†’ 6â†’# Subagents 7â†’ 8â†’A subagent is an agent that a parent agent can invoke. The parent delegates work via a tool, and the subagent executes autonomously before returning a result. 9â†’ 10â†’## How It Works 11â†’ 12â†’1. **Define a subagent** with its own model, instructions, and tools 13â†’2. **Create a tool that calls it** for the main agent to use 14â†’3. **Subagent runs independently with its own context window** 15â†’4. **Return a result** (optionally streaming progress to the UI) 16â†’5. **Control what the model sees** using `toModelOutput` to summarize 17â†’ 18â†’## When to Use Subagents 19â†’ 20â†’Subagents add latency and complexity. Use them when the benefits outweigh the costs: 21â†’ 22â†’| Use Subagents When | Avoid Subagents When | 23â†’| ----------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------ | 24â†’| Tasks require exploring large amounts of tokens | Tasks are simple and focused | 25â†’| You need to parallelize independent research | Sequential processing suffices | 26â†’| Context would grow beyond model limits | Context stays manageable | 27â†’| You want to isolate tool access by capability | All tools can safely coexist | 28â†’ 29â†’## Why Use Subagents? 30â†’ 31â†’### Offloading Context-Heavy Tasks 32â†’ 33â†’Some tasks require exploring large amounts of informationâ€”reading files, searching codebases, or researching topics. Running these in the main agent consumes context quickly, making the agent less coherent over time. 34â†’ 35â†’With subagents, you can: 36â†’ 37â†’- Spin up a dedicated agent that uses hundreds of thousands of tokens 38â†’- Have it return only a focused summary (perhaps 1,000 tokens) 39â†’- Keep your main agent's context clean and coherent 40â†’ 41â†’The subagent does the heavy lifting while the main agent stays focused on orchestration. 42â†’ 43â†’### Parallelizing Independent Work 44â†’ 45â†’For tasks like exploring a codebase, you can spawn multiple subagents to research different areas simultaneously. Each returns a summary, and the main agent synthesizes the findingsâ€”without paying the context cost of all that exploration. 46â†’ 47â†’### Specialized Orchestration 48â†’ 49â†’A less common but valid pattern is using a main agent purely for orchestration, delegating to specialized subagents for different types of work. For example: 50â†’ 51â†’- An exploration subagent with read-only tools for researching codebases 52â†’- A coding subagent with file editing tools 53â†’- An integration subagent with tools for a specific platform or API 54â†’ 55â†’This creates a clear separation of concerns, though context offloading and parallelization are the more common motivations for subagents. 56â†’ 57â†’## Basic Subagent Without Streaming 58â†’ 59â†’The simplest subagent pattern requires no special machinery. Your main agent has a tool that calls another agent in its `execute` function: 60â†’ 61â†’```ts 62â†’import { ToolLoopAgent, tool } from 'ai'; 63â†’__PROVIDER_IMPORT__; 64â†’import { z } from 'zod'; 65â†’ 66â†’// Define a subagent for research tasks 67â†’const researchSubagent = new ToolLoopAgent({ 68â†’ model: __MODEL__, 69â†’ instructions: `You are a research agent. 70â†’Summarize your findings in your final response.`, 71â†’ tools: { 72â†’ read: readFileTool, // defined elsewhere 73â†’ search: searchTool, // defined elsewhere 74â†’ }, 75â†’}); 76â†’ 77â†’// Create a tool that delegates to the subagent 78â†’const researchTool = tool({ 79â†’ description: 'Research a topic or question in depth.', 80â†’ inputSchema: z.object({ 81â†’ task: z.string().describe('The research task to complete'), 82â†’ }), 83â†’ execute: async ({ task }, { abortSignal }) => { 84â†’ const result = await researchSubagent.generate({ 85â†’ prompt: task, 86â†’ abortSignal, 87â†’ }); 88â†’ return result.text; 89â†’ }, 90â†’}); 91â†’ 92â†’// Main agent uses the research tool 93â†’const mainAgent = new ToolLoopAgent({ 94â†’ model: __MODEL__, 95â†’ instructions: 'You are a helpful assistant that can delegate research tasks.', 96â†’ tools: { 97â†’ research: researchTool, 98â†’ }, 99â†’}); 100â†’``` 101â†’ 102â†’This works well when you don't need to show the subagent's progress in the UI. The tool call blocks until the subagent completes, then returns the final text response. 103â†’ 104â†’### Handling Cancellation 105â†’ 106â†’When the user cancels a request, the `abortSignal` propagates to the subagent. Always pass it through to ensure cleanup: 107â†’ 108â†’```ts 109â†’execute: async ({ task }, { abortSignal }) => { 110â†’ const result = await researchSubagent.generate({ 111â†’ prompt: task, 112â†’ abortSignal, // Cancels subagent if main request is aborted 113â†’ }); 114â†’ return result.text; 115â†’}, 116â†’``` 117â†’ 118â†’If you abort the signal, the subagent stops executing and throws an `AbortError`. The main agent's tool execution fails, which stops the main loop. 119â†’ 120â†’To avoid errors about incomplete tool calls in subsequent messages, use `convertToModelMessages` with `ignoreIncompleteToolCalls`: 121â†’ 122â†’```ts 123â†’import { convertToModelMessages } from 'ai'; 124â†’ 125â†’const modelMessages = await convertToModelMessages(messages, { 126â†’ ignoreIncompleteToolCalls: true, 127â†’}); 128â†’``` 129â†’ 130â†’This filters out tool calls that don't have corresponding results. Learn more in the [convertToModelMessages](/docs/reference/ai-sdk-ui/convert-to-model-messages) reference. 131â†’ 132â†’## Streaming Subagent Progress 133â†’ 134â†’When you want to show incremental progress as the subagent works, use [**preliminary tool results**](/docs/ai-sdk-core/tools-and-tool-calling#preliminary-tool-results). This pattern uses a generator function that yields partial updates to the UI. 135â†’ 136â†’### How Preliminary Tool Results Work 137â†’ 138â†’Change your `execute` function from a regular function to an async generator (`async function*`). Each `yield` sends a preliminary result to the frontend: 139â†’ 140â†’```ts 141â†’execute: async function* ({ /* input */ }) { 142â†’ // ... do work ... 143â†’ yield partialResult; 144â†’ // ... do more work ... 145â†’ yield updatedResult; 146â†’} 147â†’``` 148â†’ 149â†’### Building the Complete Message 150â†’ 151â†’Each `yield` **replaces** the previous output entirely (it does not append). This means you need a way to accumulate the subagent's response into a complete message that grows over time. 152â†’ 153â†’The `readUIMessageStream` utility handles this. It reads each chunk from the stream and builds an ever-growing `UIMessage` containing all parts received so far: 154â†’ 155â†’```ts 156â†’import { readUIMessageStream, tool } from 'ai'; 157â†’import { z } from 'zod'; 158â†’ 159â†’const researchTool = tool({ 160â†’ description: 'Research a topic or question in depth.', 161â†’ inputSchema: z.object({ 162â†’ task: z.string().describe('The research task to complete'), 163â†’ }), 164â†’ execute: async function* ({ task }, { abortSignal }) { 165â†’ // Start the subagent with streaming 166â†’ const result = await researchSubagent.stream({ 167â†’ prompt: task, 168â†’ abortSignal, 169â†’ }); 170â†’ 171â†’ // Each iteration yields a complete, accumulated UIMessage 172â†’ for await (const message of readUIMessageStream({ 173â†’ stream: result.toUIMessageStream(), 174â†’ })) { 175â†’ yield message; 176â†’ } 177â†’ }, 178â†’}); 179â†’``` 180â†’ 181â†’Each yielded `message` is a complete `UIMessage` containing all the subagent's parts up to that point (text, tool calls, and tool results). The frontend simply replaces its display with each new message. 182â†’ 183â†’## Controlling What the Model Sees 184â†’ 185â†’Here's where subagents become powerful for context management. The full `UIMessage` with all the subagent's work is stored in the message history and displayed in the UI. But you can control what the main agent's model actually sees using `toModelOutput`. 186â†’ 187â†’### How It Works 188â†’ 189â†’The `toModelOutput` function maps the tool's output to the tokens sent to the model: 190â†’ 191â†’```ts 192â†’const researchTool = tool({ 193â†’ description: 'Research a topic or question in depth.', 194â†’ inputSchema: z.object({ 195â†’ task: z.string().describe('The research task to complete'), 196â†’ }), 197â†’ execute: async function* ({ task }, { abortSignal }) { 198â†’ const result = await researchSubagent.stream({ 199â†’ prompt: task, 200â†’ abortSignal, 201â†’ }); 202â†’ 203â†’ for await (const message of readUIMessageStream({ 204â†’ stream: result.toUIMessageStream(), 205â†’ })) { 206â†’ yield message; 207â†’ } 208â†’ }, 209â†’ toModelOutput: ({ output: message }) => { 210â†’ // Extract just the final text as a summary 211â†’ const lastTextPart = message?.parts.findLast(p => p.type === 'text'); 212â†’ return { 213â†’ type: 'text', 214â†’ value: lastTextPart?.text ?? 'Task completed.', 215â†’ }; 216â†’ }, 217â†’}); 218â†’``` 219â†’ 220â†’With this setup: 221â†’ 222â†’- **Users see**: The full subagent executionâ€”every tool call, every intermediate step 223â†’- **The model sees**: Just the final summary text 224â†’ 225â†’The subagent might use 100,000 tokens exploring and reasoning, but the main agent only consumes the summary. This keeps the main agent coherent and focused. 226â†’ 227â†’### Write Subagent Instructions for Summarization 228â†’ 229â†’For `toModelOutput` to extract a useful summary, your subagent must produce one. Add explicit instructions like this: 230â†’ 231â†’```ts 232â†’const researchSubagent = new ToolLoopAgent({ 233â†’ model: __MODEL__, 234â†’ instructions: `You are a research agent. Complete the task autonomously. 235â†’ 236â†’IMPORTANT: When you have finished, write a clear summary of your findings as your final response. 237â†’This summary will be returned to the main agent, so include all relevant information.`, 238â†’ tools: { 239â†’ read: readFileTool, 240â†’ search: searchTool, 241â†’ }, 242â†’}); 243â†’``` 244â†’ 245â†’Without this instruction, the subagent might not produce a comprehensive summary. It could simply say "Done", leaving `toModelOutput` with nothing useful to extract. 246â†’ 247â†’## Rendering Subagents in the UI (with useChat) 248â†’ 249â†’To display streaming progress, check the tool part's `state` and `preliminary` flag. 250â†’ 251â†’### Tool Part States 252â†’ 253â†’| State | Description | 254â†’| ------------------ | ------------------------------------------ | 255â†’| `input-streaming` | Tool input being generated | 256â†’| `input-available` | Tool ready to execute | 257â†’| `output-available` | Tool produced output (check `preliminary`) | 258â†’| `output-error` | Tool execution failed | 259â†’ 260â†’### Detecting Streaming vs Complete 261â†’ 262â†’```tsx 263â†’const hasOutput = part.state === 'output-available'; 264â†’const isStreaming = hasOutput && part.preliminary === true; 265â†’const isComplete = hasOutput && !part.preliminary; 266â†’``` 267â†’ 268â†’### Type Safety for Subagent Output 269â†’ 270â†’Export types alongside your agents for use in UI components: 271â†’ 272â†’```ts filename="lib/agents.ts" 273â†’import { ToolLoopAgent, InferAgentUIMessage } from 'ai'; 274â†’ 275â†’export const mainAgent = new ToolLoopAgent({ 276â†’ // ... configuration with researchTool 277â†’}); 278â†’ 279â†’// Export the main agent message type for the chat UI 280â†’export type MainAgentMessage = InferAgentUIMessage; 281â†’``` 282â†’ 283â†’### Render Messages and Subagent Output 284â†’ 285â†’This example uses the types defined above to render both the main agent's messages and the subagent's streamed output: 286â†’ 287â†’```tsx 288â†’'use client'; 289â†’ 290â†’import { useChat } from '@ai-sdk/react'; 291â†’import type { MainAgentMessage } from '@/lib/agents'; 292â†’ 293â†’export function Chat() { 294â†’ const { messages } = useChat(); 295â†’ 296â†’ return ( 297â†’
298â†’ {messages.map(message => 299â†’ message.parts.map((part, i) => { 300â†’ switch (part.type) { 301â†’ case 'text': 302â†’ return

{part.text}

; 303â†’ case 'tool-research': 304â†’ return ( 305â†’
306â†’ {part.state !== 'input-streaming' && ( 307â†’
Research: {part.input.task}
308â†’ )} 309â†’ {part.state === 'output-available' && ( 310â†’
311â†’ {part.output.parts.map((nestedPart, i) => { 312â†’ switch (nestedPart.type) { 313â†’ case 'text': 314â†’ return

{nestedPart.text}

; 315â†’ default: 316â†’ return null; 317â†’ } 318â†’ })} 319â†’
320â†’ )} 321â†’
322â†’ ); 323â†’ default: 324â†’ return null; 325â†’ } 326â†’ }), 327â†’ )} 328â†’
329â†’ ); 330â†’} 331â†’``` 332â†’ 333â†’## Caveats 334â†’ 335â†’### No Tool Approvals in Subagents 336â†’ 337â†’Subagent tools cannot use `needsApproval`. All tools must execute automatically without user confirmation. 338â†’ 339â†’### Subagent Context is Isolated 340â†’ 341â†’Each subagent invocation starts with a fresh context window. This is one of the key benefits of subagents: they don't inherit the accumulated context from the main agent, which is exactly what allows them to do heavy exploration without bloating the main conversation. 342â†’ 343â†’If you need to give a subagent access to the conversation history, the `messages` are available in the tool's execute function alongside `abortSignal`: 344â†’ 345â†’```ts 346â†’execute: async ({ task }, { abortSignal, messages }) => { 347â†’ const result = await researchSubagent.generate({ 348â†’ messages: [ 349â†’ ...messages, // The main agent's conversation history 350â†’ { role: 'user', content: task }, // The specific task for this invocation 351â†’ ], 352â†’ abortSignal, 353â†’ }); 354â†’ return result.text; 355â†’}, 356â†’``` 357â†’ 358â†’Use this sparingly since passing full history defeats some of the context isolation benefits. 359â†’ 360â†’### Streaming Adds Complexity 361â†’ 362â†’The basic pattern (no streaming) is simpler to implement and debug. Only add streaming when you need to show real-time progress in the UI. 363â†’ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware.      2â†’title: Overview
     3â†’description: Learn how to build agents with the AI SDK.

     5â†’
     6â†’# Agents
     7â†’
     8â†’Agents are **large language models (LLMs)** that use **tools** in a **loop** to accomplish tasks.
     9â†’
    10â†’These components work together:
    11â†’
    12â†’- **LLMs** process input and decide the next action
    13â†’- **Tools** extend capabilities beyond text generation (reading files, calling APIs, writing to databases)
    14â†’- **Loop** orchestrates execution through:
    15â†’  - **Context management** - Maintaining conversation history and deciding what the model sees (input) at each step
    16â†’  - **Stopping conditions** - Determining when the loop (task) is complete
    17â†’
    18â†’## ToolLoopAgent Class
    19â†’
    20â†’The ToolLoopAgent class handles these three components. Here's an agent that uses multiple tools in a loop to accomplish a task:
    21â†’
    22â†’```ts
    23â†’import { ToolLoopAgent, stepCountIs, tool } from 'ai';
    24â†’__PROVIDER_IMPORT__;
    25â†’import { z } from 'zod';
    26â†’
    27â†’const weatherAgent = new ToolLoopAgent({
    28â†’  model: __MODEL__,
    29â†’  tools: {
    30â†’    weather: tool({
    31â†’      description: 'Get the weather in a location (in Fahrenheit)',
    32â†’      inputSchema: z.object({
    33â†’        location: z.string().describe('The location to get the weather for'),
    34â†’      }),
    35â†’      execute: async ({ location }) => ({
    36â†’        location,
    37â†’        temperature: 72 + Math.floor(Math.random() * 21) - 10,
    38â†’      }),
    39â†’    }),
    40â†’    convertFahrenheitToCelsius: tool({
    41â†’      description: 'Convert temperature from Fahrenheit to Celsius',
    42â†’      inputSchema: z.object({
    43â†’        temperature: z.number().describe('Temperature in Fahrenheit'),
    44â†’      }),
    45â†’      execute: async ({ temperature }) => {
    46â†’        const celsius = Math.round((temperature - 32) * (5 / 9));
    47â†’        return { celsius };
    48â†’      },
    49â†’    }),
    50â†’  },
    51â†’  // Agent's default behavior is to stop after a maximum of 20 steps
    52â†’  // stopWhen: stepCountIs(20),
    53â†’});
    54â†’
    55â†’const result = await weatherAgent.generate({
    56â†’  prompt: 'What is the weather in San Francisco in celsius?',
    57â†’});
    58â†’
    59â†’console.log(result.text); // agent's final answer
    60â†’console.log(result.steps); // steps taken by the agent
    61â†’```
    62â†’
    63â†’The agent automatically:
    64â†’
    65â†’1. Calls the `weather` tool to get the temperature in Fahrenheit
    66â†’2. Calls `convertFahrenheitToCelsius` to convert it
    67â†’3. Generates a final text response with the result
    68â†’
    69â†’The Agent class handles the loop, context management, and stopping conditions.
    70â†’
    71â†’## Why Use the Agent Class?
    72â†’
    73â†’The Agent class is the recommended approach for building agents with the AI SDK because it:
    74â†’
    75â†’- **Reduces boilerplate** - Manages loops and message arrays
    76â†’- **Improves reusability** - Define once, use throughout your application
    77â†’- **Simplifies maintenance** - Single place to update agent configuration
    78â†’
    79â†’For most use cases, start with the Agent class. Use core functions (`generateText`, `streamText`) when you need explicit control over each step for complex structured workflows.
    80â†’
    81â†’## Structured Workflows
    82â†’
    83â†’Agents are flexible and powerful, but non-deterministic. When you need reliable, repeatable outcomes with explicit control flow, use core functions with structured workflow patterns combining:
    84â†’
    85â†’- Conditional statements for explicit branching
    86â†’- Standard functions for reusable logic
    87â†’- Error handling for robustness
    88â†’- Explicit control flow for predictability
    89â†’
    90â†’[Explore workflow patterns](/docs/agents/workflows) to learn more about building structured, reliable systems.
    91â†’
    92â†’## Next Steps
    93â†’
    94â†’- **[Building Agents](/docs/agents/building-agents)** - Guide to creating agents with the Agent class
    95â†’- **[Workflow Patterns](/docs/agents/workflows)** - Structured patterns using core functions for complex workflows
    96â†’- **[Loop Control](/docs/agents/loop-control)** - Execution control with stopWhen and prepareStep
    97â†’

     2â†’title: Building Agents
     3â†’description: Complete guide to creating agents with the Agent class.
     4â†’---
     5â†’
     6â†’# Building Agents
     7â†’
     8â†’The Agent class provides a structured way to encapsulate LLM configuration, tools, and behavior into reusable components. It handles the agent loop for you, allowing the LLM to call tools multiple times in sequence to accomplish complex tasks. Define agents once and use them across your application.
     9â†’
    10â†’## Why Use the ToolLoopAgent Class?
    11â†’
    12â†’When building AI applications, you often need to:
    13â†’
    14â†’- **Reuse configurations** - Same model settings, tools, and prompts across different parts of your application
    15â†’- **Maintain consistency** - Ensure the same behavior and capabilities throughout your codebase
    16â†’- **Simplify API routes** - Reduce boilerplate in your endpoints
    17â†’- **Type safety** - Get full TypeScript support for your agent's tools and outputs
    18â†’
    19â†’The ToolLoopAgent class provides a single place to define your agent's behavior.
    20â†’
    21â†’## Creating an Agent
    22â†’
    23â†’Define an agent by instantiating the ToolLoopAgent class with your desired configuration:
    24â†’
    25â†’```ts
    26â†’import { ToolLoopAgent } from 'ai';
    27â†’__PROVIDER_IMPORT__;
    28â†’
    29â†’const myAgent = new ToolLoopAgent({
    30â†’  model: __MODEL__,
    31â†’  instructions: 'You are a helpful assistant.',
    32â†’  tools: {
    33â†’    // Your tools here
    34â†’  },
    35â†’});
    36â†’```
    37â†’
    38â†’## Configuration Options
    39â†’
    40â†’The Agent class accepts all the same settings as `generateText` and `streamText`. Configure:
    41â†’
    42â†’### Model and System Instructions
    43â†’
    44â†’```ts
    45â†’import { ToolLoopAgent } from 'ai';
    46â†’__PROVIDER_IMPORT__;
    47â†’
    48â†’const agent = new ToolLoopAgent({
    49â†’  model: __MODEL__,
    50â†’  instructions: 'You are an expert software engineer.',
    51â†’});
    52â†’```
    53â†’
    54â†’### Tools
    55â†’
    56â†’Provide tools that the agent can use to accomplish tasks:
    57â†’
    58â†’```ts
    59â†’import { ToolLoopAgent, tool } from 'ai';
    60â†’__PROVIDER_IMPORT__;
    61â†’import { z } from 'zod';
    62â†’
    63â†’const codeAgent = new ToolLoopAgent({
    64â†’  model: __MODEL__,
    65â†’  tools: {
    66â†’    runCode: tool({
    67â†’      description: 'Execute Python code',
    68â†’      inputSchema: z.object({
    69â†’        code: z.string(),
    70â†’      }),
    71â†’      execute: async ({ code }) => {
    72â†’        // Execute code and return result
    73â†’        return { output: 'Code executed successfully' };
    74â†’      },
    75â†’    }),
    76â†’  },
    77â†’});
    78â†’```
    79â†’
    80â†’### Loop Control
    81â†’
    82â†’By default, agents run for 20 steps (`stopWhen: stepCountIs(20)`). In each step, the model either generates text or calls a tool. If it generates text, the agent completes. If it calls a tool, the AI SDK executes that tool.
    83â†’
    84â†’To let agents call multiple tools in sequence, configure `stopWhen` to allow more steps. After each tool execution, the agent triggers a new generation where the model can call another tool or generate text:
    85â†’
    86â†’```ts
    87â†’import { ToolLoopAgent, stepCountIs } from 'ai';
    88â†’__PROVIDER_IMPORT__;
    89â†’
    90â†’const agent = new ToolLoopAgent({
    91â†’  model: __MODEL__,
    92â†’  stopWhen: stepCountIs(20), // Allow up to 20 steps
    93â†’});
    94â†’```
    95â†’
    96â†’Each step represents one generation (which results in either text or a tool call). The loop continues until:
    97â†’
    98â†’- A finish reasoning other than tool-calls is returned, or
    99â†’- A tool that is invoked does not have an execute function, or
   100â†’- A tool call needs approval, or
   101â†’- A stop condition is met
   102â†’
   103â†’You can combine multiple conditions:
   104â†’
   105â†’```ts
   106â†’import { ToolLoopAgent, stepCountIs } from 'ai';
   107â†’__PROVIDER_IMPORT__;
   108â†’
   109â†’const agent = new ToolLoopAgent({
   110â†’  model: __MODEL__,
   111â†’  stopWhen: [
   112â†’    stepCountIs(20), // Maximum 20 steps
   113â†’    yourCustomCondition(), // Custom logic for when to stop
   114â†’  ],
   115â†’});
   116â†’```
   117â†’
   118â†’Learn more about [loop control and stop conditions](/docs/agents/loop-control).
   119â†’
   120â†’### Tool Choice
   121â†’
   122â†’Control how the agent uses tools:
   123â†’
   124â†’```ts
   125â†’import { ToolLoopAgent } from 'ai';
   126â†’__PROVIDER_IMPORT__;
   127â†’
   128â†’const agent = new ToolLoopAgent({
   129â†’  model: __MODEL__,
   130â†’  tools: {
   131â†’    // your tools here
   132â†’  },
   133â†’  toolChoice: 'required', // Force tool use
   134â†’  // or toolChoice: 'none' to disable tools
   135â†’  // or toolChoice: 'auto' (default) to let the model decide
   136â†’});
   137â†’```
   138â†’
   139â†’You can also force the use of a specific tool:
   140â†’
   141â†’```ts
   142â†’import { ToolLoopAgent } from 'ai';
   143â†’__PROVIDER_IMPORT__;
   144â†’
   145â†’const agent = new ToolLoopAgent({
   146â†’  model: __MODEL__,
   147â†’  tools: {
   148â†’    weather: weatherTool,
   149â†’    cityAttractions: attractionsTool,
   150â†’  },
   151â†’  toolChoice: {
   152â†’    type: 'tool',
   153â†’    toolName: 'weather', // Force the weather tool to be used
   154â†’  },
   155â†’});
   156â†’```
   157â†’
   158â†’### Structured Output
   159â†’
   160â†’Define structured output schemas:
   161â†’
   162â†’```ts
   163â†’import { ToolLoopAgent, Output, stepCountIs } from 'ai';
   164â†’__PROVIDER_IMPORT__;
   165â†’import { z } from 'zod';
   166â†’
   167â†’const analysisAgent = new ToolLoopAgent({
   168â†’  model: __MODEL__,
   169â†’  output: Output.object({
   170â†’    schema: z.object({
   171â†’      sentiment: z.enum(['positive', 'neutral', 'negative']),
   172â†’      summary: z.string(),
   173â†’      keyPoints: z.array(z.string()),
   174â†’    }),
   175â†’  }),
   176â†’  stopWhen: stepCountIs(10),
   177â†’});
   178â†’
   179â†’const { output } = await analysisAgent.generate({
   180â†’  prompt: 'Analyze customer feedback from the last quarter',
   181â†’});
   182â†’```
   183â†’
   184â†’## Define Agent Behavior with System Instructions
   185â†’
   186â†’System instructions define your agent's behavior, personality, and constraints. They set the context for all interactions and guide how the agent responds to user queries and uses tools.
   187â†’
   188â†’### Basic System Instructions
   189â†’
   190â†’Set the agent's role and expertise:
   191â†’
   192â†’```ts
   193â†’const agent = new ToolLoopAgent({
   194â†’  model: __MODEL__,
   195â†’  instructions:
   196â†’    'You are an expert data analyst. You provide clear insights from complex data.',
   197â†’});
   198â†’```
   199â†’
   200â†’### Detailed Behavioral Instructions
   201â†’
   202â†’Provide specific guidelines for agent behavior:
   203â†’
   204â†’```ts
   205â†’const codeReviewAgent = new ToolLoopAgent({
   206â†’  model: __MODEL__,
   207â†’  instructions: `You are a senior software engineer conducting code reviews.
   208â†’
   209â†’  Your approach:
   210â†’  - Focus on security vulnerabilities first
   211â†’  - Identify performance bottlenecks
   212â†’  - Suggest improvements for readability and maintainability
   213â†’  - Be constructive and educational in your feedback
   214â†’  - Always explain why something is an issue and how to fix it`,
   215â†’});
   216â†’```
   217â†’
   218â†’### Constrain Agent Behavior
   219â†’
   220â†’Set boundaries and ensure consistent behavior:
   221â†’
   222â†’```ts
   223â†’const customerSupportAgent = new ToolLoopAgent({
   224â†’  model: __MODEL__,
   225â†’  instructions: `You are a customer support specialist for an e-commerce platform.
   226â†’
   227â†’  Rules:
   228â†’  - Never make promises about refunds without checking the policy
   229â†’  - Always be empathetic and professional
   230â†’  - If you don't know something, say so and offer to escalate
   231â†’  - Keep responses concise and actionable
   232â†’  - Never share internal company information`,
   233â†’  tools: {
   234â†’    checkOrderStatus,
   235â†’    lookupPolicy,
   236â†’    createTicket,
   237â†’  },
   238â†’});
   239â†’```
   240â†’
   241â†’### Tool Usage Instructions
   242â†’
   243â†’Guide how the agent should use available tools:
   244â†’
   245â†’```ts
   246â†’const researchAgent = new ToolLoopAgent({
   247â†’  model: __MODEL__,
   248â†’  instructions: `You are a research assistant with access to search and document tools.
   249â†’
   250â†’  When researching:
   251â†’  1. Always start with a broad search to understand the topic
   252â†’  2. Use document analysis for detailed information
   253â†’  3. Cross-reference multiple sources before drawing conclusions
   254â†’  4. Cite your sources when presenting information
   255â†’  5. If information conflicts, present both viewpoints`,
   256â†’  tools: {
   257â†’    webSearch,
   258â†’    analyzeDocument,
   259â†’    extractQuotes,
   260â†’  },
   261â†’});
   262â†’```
   263â†’
   264â†’### Format and Style Instructions
   265â†’
   266â†’Control the output format and communication style:
   267â†’
   268â†’```ts
   269â†’const technicalWriterAgent = new ToolLoopAgent({
   270â†’  model: __MODEL__,
   271â†’  instructions: `You are a technical documentation writer.
   272â†’
   273â†’  Writing style:
   274â†’  - Use clear, simple language
   275â†’  - Avoid jargon unless necessary
   276â†’  - Structure information with headers and bullet points
   277â†’  - Include code examples where relevant
   278â†’  - Write in second person ("you" instead of "the user")
   279â†’
   280â†’  Always format responses in Markdown.`,
   281â†’});
   282â†’```
   283â†’
   284â†’## Using an Agent
   285â†’
   286â†’Once defined, you can use your agent in three ways:
   287â†’
   288â†’### Generate Text
   289â†’
   290â†’Use `generate()` for one-time text generation:
   291â†’
   292â†’```ts
   293â†’const result = await myAgent.generate({
   294â†’  prompt: 'What is the weather like?',
   295â†’});
   296â†’
   297â†’console.log(result.text);
   298â†’```
   299â†’
   300â†’### Stream Text
   301â†’
   302â†’Use `stream()` for streaming responses:
   303â†’
   304â†’```ts
   305â†’const result = await myAgent.stream({
   306â†’  prompt: 'Tell me a story',
   307â†’});
   308â†’
   309â†’for await (const chunk of result.textStream) {
   310â†’  console.log(chunk);
   311â†’}
   312â†’```
   313â†’
   314â†’### Respond to UI Messages
   315â†’
   316â†’Use `createAgentUIStreamResponse()` to create API responses for client applications:
   317â†’
   318â†’```ts
   319â†’// In your API route (e.g., app/api/chat/route.ts)
   320â†’import { createAgentUIStreamResponse } from 'ai';
   321â†’
   322â†’export async function POST(request: Request) {
   323â†’  const { messages } = await request.json();
   324â†’
   325â†’  return createAgentUIStreamResponse({
   326â†’    agent: myAgent,
   327â†’    uiMessages: messages,
   328â†’  });
   329â†’}
   330â†’```
   331â†’
   332â†’### Track Step Progress
   333â†’
   334â†’Use `onStepFinish` to track each step's progress, including token usage:
   335â†’
   336â†’```ts
   337â†’const result = await myAgent.generate({
   338â†’  prompt: 'Research and summarize the latest AI trends',
   339â†’  onStepFinish: async ({ usage, finishReason, toolCalls }) => {
   340â†’    console.log('Step completed:', {
   341â†’      inputTokens: usage.inputTokens,
   342â†’      outputTokens: usage.outputTokens,
   343â†’      finishReason,
   344â†’      toolsUsed: toolCalls?.map(tc => tc.toolName),
   345â†’    });
   346â†’  },
   347â†’});
   348â†’```
   349â†’
   350â†’You can also define `onStepFinish` in the constructor for agent-wide tracking. When both constructor and method callbacks are provided, both are called (constructor first, then the method callback):
   351â†’
   352â†’```ts
   353â†’const agent = new ToolLoopAgent({
   354â†’  model: __MODEL__,
   355â†’  onStepFinish: async ({ usage }) => {
   356â†’    // Agent-wide logging
   357â†’    console.log('Agent step:', usage.totalTokens);
   358â†’  },
   359â†’});
   360â†’
   361â†’// Method-level callback runs after constructor callback
   362â†’const result = await agent.generate({
   363â†’  prompt: 'Hello',
   364â†’  onStepFinish: async ({ usage }) => {
   365â†’    // Per-call tracking (e.g., for billing)
   366â†’    await trackUsage(usage);
   367â†’  },
   368â†’});
   369â†’```
   370â†’
   371â†’## End-to-end Type Safety
   372â†’
   373â†’You can infer types for your agent's `UIMessage`s:
   374â†’
   375â†’```ts
   376â†’import { ToolLoopAgent, InferAgentUIMessage } from 'ai';
   377â†’
   378â†’const myAgent = new ToolLoopAgent({
   379â†’  // ... configuration
   380â†’});
   381â†’
   382â†’// Infer the UIMessage type for UI components or persistence
   383â†’export type MyAgentUIMessage = InferAgentUIMessage<typeof myAgent>;
   384â†’```
   385â†’
   386â†’Use this type in your client components with `useChat`:
   387â†’
   388â†’```tsx filename="components/chat.tsx"
   389â†’'use client';
   390â†’
   391â†’import { useChat } from '@ai-sdk/react';
   392â†’import type { MyAgentUIMessage } from '@/agent/my-agent';
   393â†’
   394â†’export function Chat() {
   395â†’  const { messages } = useChat<MyAgentUIMessage>();
   396â†’  // Full type safety for your messages and tools
   397â†’}
   398â†’```
   399â†’
   400â†’## Next Steps
   401â†’
   402â†’Now that you understand building agents, you can:
   403â†’
   404â†’- Explore [workflow patterns](/docs/agents/workflows) for structured patterns using core functions
   405â†’- Learn about [loop control](/docs/agents/loop-control) for advanced execution control
   406â†’- See [manual loop examples](/cookbook/node/manual-agent-loop) for custom workflow implementations
   407â†’

1â†’import { anthropic } from "@ai-sdk/anthropic";
     2â†’import { type InferAgentUIMessage, stepCountIs, ToolLoopAgent } from "ai";
     3â†’import {
     4â†’  main_agent_prompt,
     5â†’  manual_mode_agent_prompt,
     6â†’} from "@/lib/langfuse/prompt";
     7â†’import { getPromptWithFallback } from "@/lib/langfuse/prompt-fetcher";
     8â†’import logger from "@/utils/logger";
     9â†’import {
    10â†’  editImageTool,
    11â†’  generateAudioTool,
    12â†’  generateImageTool,
    13â†’  generateVideoTool,
    14â†’  removeBackgroundTool,
    15â†’  resizeImageTool,
    16â†’  upscaleImageTool,
    17â†’  videoAddAudioTool,
    18â†’  videoLipSyncTool,
    19â†’  videoReframeTool,
    20â†’  videoSpeedChangeTool,
    21â†’  videoStitchTool,
    22â†’  videoTrimTool,
    23â†’  videoUpscalerTool,
    24â†’} from "./tools";
    25â†’
    26â†’import {
    27â†’  type MediaAgentCallOptions,
    28â†’  mediaAgentCallOptionsSchema,
    29â†’} from "./types";
    30â†’
    31â†’/**
    32â†’ * Tools available to the media agent.
    33â†’ * Exported for validation in the route handler.
    34â†’ */
    35â†’export const mediaAgentTools = {
    36â†’  generateImage: generateImageTool,
    37â†’  generateVideo: generateVideoTool,
    38â†’  editImage: editImageTool,
    39â†’  generateAudio: generateAudioTool,
    40â†’  removeBackground: removeBackgroundTool,
    41â†’  resizeImage: resizeImageTool,
    42â†’  upscaleImage: upscaleImageTool,
    43â†’  // Video editing tools
    44â†’  videoLipSync: videoLipSyncTool,
    45â†’  videoAddAudio: videoAddAudioTool,
    46â†’  videoTrim: videoTrimTool,
    47â†’  videoSpeedChange: videoSpeedChangeTool,
    48â†’  videoStitch: videoStitchTool,
    49â†’  videoReframe: videoReframeTool,
    50â†’  videoUpscaler: videoUpscalerTool,
    51â†’};
    52â†’
    53â†’/**
    54â†’ * Media Agent - A specialized AI creative assistant for image generation,
    55â†’ * video creation, audio production, and media editing tasks.
    56â†’ *
    57â†’ * Uses ToolLoopAgent pattern for cleaner architecture and built-in loop control.
    58â†’ */
    59â†’export const mediaAgent = new ToolLoopAgent<
    60â†’  MediaAgentCallOptions,
    61â†’  typeof mediaAgentTools
    62â†’>({
    63â†’  id: "media-agent",
    64â†’  model: anthropic("claude-haiku-4-5"),
    65â†’  tools: mediaAgentTools,
    66â†’  maxOutputTokens: 10_000,
    67â†’  stopWhen: stepCountIs(20),
    68â†’  callOptionsSchema: mediaAgentCallOptionsSchema,
    69â†’  experimental_telemetry: { isEnabled: true },
    70â†’
    71â†’  /**
    72â†’   * Prepare the call with dynamic system prompt based on mode (auto vs manual).
    73â†’   * Receives default settings and returns overrides for this call.
    74â†’   * IMPORTANT: Must pass through `prompt` from input to output (SDK uses prompt, not messages).
    75â†’   */
    76â†’  prepareCall: async ({ options, model, tools, maxOutputTokens, prompt }) => {
    77â†’    const isManualMode = options && !options.isAutoMode;
    78â†’
    79â†’    let baseSystemPrompt: string;
    80â†’
    81â†’    if (isManualMode && options) {
    82â†’      // Manual mode: use user's selected models
    83â†’      const manualPromptFallback = manual_mode_agent_prompt(
    84â†’        options.imageModel,
    85â†’        options.videoModel,
    86â†’      );
    87â†’      baseSystemPrompt = await getPromptWithFallback(
    88â†’        "manual_mode_agent_prompt",
    89â†’        manualPromptFallback,
    90â†’      );
    91â†’      logger.info(
    92â†’        `Manual mode enabled - Image: ${options.imageModel}, Video: ${options.videoModel}`,
    93â†’      );
    94â†’    } else {
    95â†’      // Auto mode: agent selects best models
    96â†’      baseSystemPrompt = await getPromptWithFallback(
    97â†’        "main_agent_prompt",
    98â†’        main_agent_prompt,
    99â†’      );
   100â†’      logger.info("Auto mode enabled - agent will select best models");
   101â†’    }
   102â†’
   103â†’    // Merge with user's custom system instructions if provided
   104â†’    const globalInstructions = options?.globalSystemInstructions?.trim();
   105â†’    const mergedInstructions = [
   106â†’      baseSystemPrompt,
   107â†’      globalInstructions?.length
   108â†’        ? `\n\n<user-system-instructions>\n${globalInstructions}\n</user-system-instructions>\n`
   109â†’        : "",
   110â†’    ].join("");
   111â†’
   112â†’    // Return the updated settings - pass through required fields including prompt
   113â†’    // NOTE: createAgentUIStreamResponse passes model messages as `prompt`, not `messages`
   114â†’    return {
   115â†’      model,
   116â†’      tools,
   117â†’      maxOutputTokens,
   118â†’      instructions: mergedInstructions,
   119â†’      prompt, // CRITICAL: Must pass through prompt from createAgentUIStreamResponse
   120â†’    };
   121â†’  },
   122â†’});
   123â†’
   124â†’/**
   125â†’ * Type-safe UIMessage type for the media agent.
   126â†’ * Use this with useChat<MediaAgentUIMessage>() for type-safe tool results.
   127â†’ */
   128â†’export type MediaAgentUIMessage = InferAgentUIMessage<typeof mediaAgent>;
